reasoning:
The benchmark is the 30-day average of 'Violence against civilians' over the past 360 days, which stands at 24.5. This 360-day period (January 2025 to January 2026) includes the Philippines' mid-term elections in May 2025, which ACLED noted were 'marred by a countrywide surge in violence', as well as the arrest of former President Rodrigo Duterte in mid-2025. Because these major conflict events caused massive, temporary spikes in violence, the 12-month average (mean) is pulled upwards, meaning 'normal' non-election months naturally fall comfortably below this mean. 

For the first resolution period (Dec 26, 2025 - Jan 25, 2026), much of the duration covers the New Year holiday lull where government work and operations were suspended, further reducing the likelihood of exceeding the 24.5 threshold. The second period (Jan 18 - Feb 17) will not be impacted by holidays but still lacks the major conflict catalysts of mid-2025, thus should also remain below the yearly average. Using a Poisson distribution assumption, if the true 'non-election' monthly mean is around 20 events, the probability of hitting 25+ events is roughly 15-20%. Incorporating some uncertainty for potential short-term escalations or coding anomalies from ACLED's October 2025 methodology update, my estimates are 26% and 35%, scaling towards 0.5 for the more distant date.
